channel.title: Bloomberg Green Global
channel.description: Últimos artigos de Bloomberg Green Global

item.title: Trump’s Alaska Oil, Gas Lease Auction Draws Just Two Bidders
item.description:
Only two bidders participated in the Trump administration’s auction for drilling leases in Alaska’s Arctic National Wildlife Refuge as big oil companies sat out the sale.

The Alaska Industrial Development and Export Authority, a state agency, and Anchorage-based Hex Energy were the sole entrants in the auction for nearly 690,000 acres of oil and gas drilling rights. It was the Interior Department’s first lease sale in the refuge since lifting drilling restrictions imposed by former President Joe Biden.

It was a key test of the oil industry’s appetite to drill in the rugged frozen tundra, long prized for its oil and gas potential but burdened by political uncertainty, environmental opposition and logistical challenges.

The sale was the first of four lease sales required under President Donald Trump’s tax-and-spending law, the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, which mandates at least four auctions in the area by 2035. It comes as Trump seeks to make tapping Alaska’s vast natural resources a key part of his energy agenda.

Combined, the Industrial Development and Export Authority and Hex Energy bought leases for five tracts through the sale.

Trump signed legislation during his first term ending a four-decade ban on energy development in the refuge, which is estimated to contain as much as 11.8 billion barrels of recoverable oil. The protected area occupies a section of northeastern Alaska roughly the size of South Carolina.

Major oil companies largely steered clear of the first two lease sales that followed. An auction held weeks before Trump left office in January 2021 drew bids from two oil developers and Alaska’s state-owned economic development company. A second sale in January 2025 under the Biden administration resulted in zero bids, though oil industry representatives and Alaska officials argued restrictive lease terms artificially discouraged interest.

Some analysts are skeptical about the prospects for drilling in the region.

“Production will be challenging, and the prospect that permits will be canceled as soon as a new administration takes over is likely given that this is an easy way for a new administration to make a show of its environmental commitment,” said Ellen Wald, a senior fellow with the Atlantic Council Global Energy Center and the president of Transversal Consulting.

Cooper Freeman, Alaska director for environmental group Center for Biological Diversity, called the auction a “flop of a lease sale.”

“This stunning yet predictable failure shows that drilling in this pristine landscape is a dead end for the fossil fuel industry,” Freeman said in an emailed statement.

Still, a March lease sale in the National Petroleum Reserve in Alaska drew a record $163 million in bids from established operators like ConocoPhillips, as well as companies such as Exxon Mobil Corp., which last drilled an exploratory well in the state in the early 1990s. Shell Plc, which abandoned Arctic exploration after a costly unsuccessful search for crude waters north of Alaska, partnered with Repsol SA to secure over 40 leases.

Read more: Alaska’s Oil Revival Draws Energy Industry Back to the Arctic

Environmental groups and some indigenous communities, including Gwich’in, which consider the coastal plain sacred, argue drilling threatens Arctic foxes, polar bears, caribou, musk oxen and migratory birds.

“The diverse and sacred landscape of the Arctic Refuge is unlike any other and should never be sacrificed for oil and gas drilling,” said America Fitzpatrick, a program director with the League of Conservation Voters. “Any companies considering drilling in the Arctic Refuge would be doing so against the majority of people who support protecting this critical landscape.”

Meanwhile, local leaders, including those in Kaktovik, the only village within the refuge, support development, arguing it’s necessary for the region’s economic well-being.

Read More: Trump Reopens Oil Drilling Alaska Arctic Wildlife Refuge

Alaska crude production has steadily declined from a peak of 2 million barrels a day in 1988 to roughly 417,000 barrels a day in March, according to the Energy Information Administration. The agency projects output will rise to 450,000 barrels a day this year and 500,000 barrels a day in 2027 as new developments begin producing.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Liao

item.title: US Says Antares’ Small Nuclear Reactor Reaches ‘Criticality’
item.description:
The Trump administration announced a small modular nuclear reactor reached a critical milestone Thursday.

The small reactor being developed by Antares Nuclear Inc. participating in an Energy Department pilot program reached “criticality,” the agency said in a statement. The milestone occurs when a nuclear chain reaction becomes self-sustaining enough to produce a steady release of energy.

The achievement shows that the Trump administration’s effort to remove regulatory barriers is helping demonstrate the viability of new nuclear technologies.

Read More: US Unveils Program Aimed at Expediting Advanced Reactor Designs

The White House is pushing for wider deployment of both large, conventional reactors and smaller ones from companies like Antares. While the Antares system is still far from being used in a commercial capacity, achieving criticality is a notable step toward that goal. The company, which is initially targeting military applications, said it expects to see its systems deployed in the field by the end of 2028.

“This initial criticality is the first step on a roadmap toward producing electricity,” Jordan Bramble, Antares’ chief executive officer, said during a video briefing Friday.

The Energy Department has set a goal of achieving the milestone in at least three test reactors by July 4 and selected projects from Antares, Oklo Inc., Aalo Atomics Inc., Atomic Alchemy Inc., Deep Fission Inc., Last Energy Inc., Natura Resources LLC, Radiant Energy Inc., Terrestrial Energy Inc., and Valar Atomics Inc. to participate in a program designed to expedite development and authorization of reactors.
item.dc:creator: Ari Natter, Will Wade

item.title: Biodiversity Decline Threatens Sovereign Credit Ratings, Study Says
item.description:
A growing chorus of economists, analysts and academics is raising concerns about the impact of environmental changes on sovereign credit ratings.

A study published Friday in the journal Nature Ecology & Evolution led by researchers at the University of Sussex, University of Sheffield and Heriot-Watt University found that a partial ecological collapse would be enough to increase borrowing costs for almost two dozen countries. The resulting impact would raise annual interest payments by $162 billion and cut the global gross domestic product by $2 trillion, according to the report.

Healthy biodiversity supports economic activity in numerous ways, from insects pollinating crops to marine ecosystems that underpin the seafood industry. Losing these ecosystem services would carry significant financial costs. In their study, the researchers modeled scenarios involving disruptions to key ecosystem services, including wild pollination, marine fisheries and tropical timber production.

“Everyone is sort of coming to the realization that nature is important and biodiversity is important, but people aren’t really sure how and what it means for them and their investments,” said Matt Burke, who co-authored the report and lectures at the Sheffield University Management School.

The researchers defined a partial ecosystem collapse as a 90% reduction in wild pollination services, a 90% reduction in total catch biomass from marine fisheries and a conversion of 88% of tropical forest into grassland or shrubland.

The paper found that Angola, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Bangladesh, Pakistan, Ethiopia and Vietnam would suffer some of the largest hits to economic growth. Along with those countries, Egypt and India were among the countries most vulnerable to debt defaults, while China and Malaysia would be vulnerable to the biggest rating change. The study says sovereign credit ratings mostly ignore these risks, meaning that “markets may be mispricing, mismanaging and misallocating $83 trillion of financial assets.”

Rising sovereign debt levels are a growing concern for investors. A country’s borrowing costs influence its ability to fund public services, build infrastructure and invest in long-term priorities such as climate adaptation. As rates rise, governments have less fiscal room to finance new projects and respond to economic shocks. Higher borrowing costs have also played a role in past debt crises, amplifying financial stress and slowing economic growth.

“The 2008 global financial crisis was an example of what happens when markets and ratings agencies ignore new risks,” said Pati Klusak, a professor at Edinburgh Business School at Heriot-Watt University and co-author of the report. “The financial system risks once again sleepwalking into a catastrophe.”

The research team used artificial intelligence to develop machine-learning models that integrate environmental science into credit assessments. The paper calls on regulators, central banks and credit-rating agencies to factor nature- and climate-related vulnerabilities into standard financial assessments.

“What I would like to see more of is attempts by asset managers, asset owners and people in financial markets to try and do more to incorporate that science into their own decision-making,” Burke said. “Finance has to adapt to the science, not the other way around.”

The study is the latest in a series of reports this year attempting to quantify how climate change and nature loss affect sovereign borrowing costs. Earlier this year, researchers at the University of Oxford and London School of Economics and Political Science found that ecosystem degradation pushed up government bond yields, boosting debt-servicing expenses. The effects were most pronounced in emerging markets across Africa and Asia, where biodiversity loss had an outsize influence on debt markets.

Fitch Ratings has also warned that climate-related threats are likely to play a larger role in corporate credit assessments. This week, analysts at Bloomberg Intelligence projected $20 trillion in climate-related spending over the next decade and said that federal disaster-recovery support may drop to levels that jeopardize funding in 26 US states.
item.dc:creator: Olivia Raimonde

item.title: India Power Giant May Revive Small Coal Plants to Aid Solar
item.description:
India’s largest power producer NTPC Ltd. is looking to bring back sub-critical coal generators able to run at low capacity, to accommodate more renewables in the grid.

The New-Delhi based firm is seeking vendors who could build plants of maximum 250 megawatts capacity, that could operate at as low as 25% of their potential, it said in an emailed statement on Friday.

Sub-critical technologies are widely seen as more polluting than their high-performing, but less resilient, counterparts. NTPC said it’s looking for new-generation sub-critical units that could help in grid stability.

Inadequate hydropower, gas power and battery storage capacity have put the burden on the country’s coal-fired fleet to operate flexibly, requiring it to reduce its output when clean energy is abundant. But state of the art ultra-supercritical plants deteriorate faster when they don’t work at full capacity, and many operators are hesitant to run them below potential. That has led to the curtailment of solar power in times of low demand.

Read More: India Taps Coal Plants to Make Room For More Solar in The Grid

State-run NTPC’s “entire thermal fleet is already operating at a technical minimum load of 55% during low demand periods,” also participating in ancillary services to accommodate renewables, particularly during solar hours, according to the statement.
item.dc:creator: Rajesh Kumar Singh

item.title: India Unveils Ethanol-Rich Fuel as It Seeks to Reduce Oil Imports
item.description:
India has launched a new high-ethanol fuel blend in the market, its latest effort to diversify supplies and reduce the country’s reliance on imported oil.

Oil Minister Hardeep Puri introduced the fuel Friday at an Indian Oil Corp.’s outlet in New Delhi. It will initially be sold at 50 fuel stations across the country.

The South Asian nation currently sells 20% ethanol-blended gasoline, or E20, across its retail fuel network. The new blend, called E85, will contain about 85% ethanol and pollute less than its conventional counterparts. The E85 fuel will be priced 20 rupees lower than E20 gasoline, the minister said.

India currently has ethanol production capacity of 19 billion liters, Puri said. The current blending program uses 11.5 billion liters, he said. E85 fuel will help boost demand for ethanol.

India’s transition to electric mobility has so far been slow due to the high cost of vehicles and limited charging infrastructure. Policymakers view ethanol-compatible vehicles as a complementary pathway to cutting oil consumption, requiring less infrastructure investment than a nationwide electric-vehicle charging network.

Ethanol is derived from agricultural feed stocks such as sugarcane, rice and corn, and is used to produce alcoholic beverages, pharmaceuticals and chemicals. Still, its single biggest usage in India is fuel blends, which in higher concentrations could boost demand for farming byproducts and support rural incomes.

India’s consumption of gasoline, mostly used for two-wheelers, has risen by an annual average of 9% over the last five financial years, lifting oil imports.

Read More: India Set to Boost Biogas Prices, Subsidies in New Program

Earlier this week, Puri presented new E85-compatible variants of Hero MotoCorp Ltd.’s motorcycles and Maruti Suzuki India Ltd.’s Wagon R hatchback, indicating the government’s intent to help build a transport ecosystem to support higher ethanol use. The government is mulling policy measures to accelerate the adoption of hybrid-fuel vehicles, according to Puri. The fuel will be available at as many as 500 pumps by the end of the year, he said.

The new fuel enters the market amid lingering concerns over the impact of ethanol blends on mileage and engine durability. The oil ministry said last year that E20 gasoline offers better acceleration, improved ride quality and lower carbon emissions compared with conventional fuel, benefits the ministry says could be amplified with higher ethanol blends.
item.dc:creator: Rakesh Sharma, Alisha Sachdev

item.title: How Extreme Heat Will Factor Into World Cup Games
item.description: Some teams will face a much hotter path to the finals.
item.dc:creator: Sharon Chen, Emma Court, Elena Mejia, David Ingold, Joe Wertz, Eric Roston

item.title: US Public Transit Bounces Back as War-Fueled Gas Prices Remain High
item.description:
As the Iran war enters its fourth month, ridership on US public transit systems is rising as gasoline prices remain near historic highs.

Weekday commutes on Los Angeles County’s Metro system, for instance, jumped nearly 8% between January and May. “With gas prices in April averaging roughly $5.70 per gallon, the ability to travel across the county for just $1.75 makes rail an increasingly attractive and cost-effective alternative,” Maya Pogoda, senior director of communications for Los Angeles’s Metro system, said in an email.

Transportation agencies in Boston, Chicago and other cities also report growing ridership. Experts said it remains unclear to what extent gas prices are driving a switch to public transit versus other factors, such as return-to-office mandates and the ongoing recovery from the pandemic. But a May report from the New York Federal Reserve found that low- and moderate-income households have decreased their consumption of gasoline as pump prices spike, “potentially by carpooling or substituting to public transit where available.”

Studies have correlated a rise in gas prices with an increase in public transit use. Hiroyuki Iseki, an associate professor of urban studies and planning at the University of Maryland, said his research has shown that every 10% increase in gas prices over the course of a year results in up to a 1.2% rise in public transit demand.

“The cost pressure accumulates over time,” he said in an email. “Some segments of people will be forced to consider the trade-off between transportation costs, housing costs, convenience [and] activities to conduct that generate the need to travel.”

California’s gas costs are 41% higher than the US average — regular gas in the Golden State hovers above $6 a gallon with premium fuel edging toward $7 — and the state has seen notable increases in public transit use. In San Diego County, light rail ridership jumped nearly 18% between February and April compared to an 11% increase for the same period last year, according to the latest available data.

In Northern California, the Bay Area Rapid Transit (BART) train system saw a nearly 11% rise in weekday ridership between January and May. April ridership surged 21% on Caltrain, the commuter railroad that links Silicon Valley to San Francisco, compared to the previous year.

Chicago experienced an 11% increase between January and April with more interest from lower-income customers, according to a spokesperson for the city’s transit authority. Ridership on Boston’s rail line rose 13%.

Read more: Global Cities Relearn an Old Lesson: How to Weather an Oil Shock

With the Iran war at a stalemate, gas prices are expected to remain high throughout 2026. Even so, Iseki said drivers are loath to give up their wheels. After the oil shocks of 2008 and 2022, drivers largely returned to old habits when gas prices declined, according to researchers.

Experts said that though this is the second sudden run-up in gas prices in four years and comes amid persistent inflation and geopolitical upheaval, it may not result in a significant switch to public transit — though it could cut driving. That’s because people now have more alternatives, according to David King, an associate professor in the School of Geographical Sciences and Urban Planning at Arizona State University.

“If we don’t drive, some trips might turn to transit, some trips might turn to work-from-home, some trips might turn to delivery, some are going to be biking or walking,” he said, noting people can also keep driving but eliminate their gas bill by switching to an electric vehicle.

Brian Taylor, a professor of urban planning and public policy at the University of California at Los Angeles, said drivers are more likely to leave their cars at home in areas with robust public transportation. He noted that an increase in ridership on those systems is easier to correlate with the effect of fuel prices.

Taylor said the impact of high gas prices will vary across the country and depends in part on how convenient the public transit system is in a particular city, particularly for suburban commuters. “If you’re driving to work but you have a very high quality transit system as an alternative and parking is difficult, some of those people will stick with it,” he said.
item.dc:creator: Todd Woody

item.title: Chinese Solar Firms Look Beyond Earth for Growth
item.description: Years of crippling oversupply are forcing the industry to explore new markets.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Asia’s ‘Ukraine Moment’ Is Supercharging the Energy Transition
item.description:
It's been more than three months since the US attacked Iran, leading to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz that carries 20% of the world's oil and gas. The shock to energy markets has been so intense that some countries are taking longer-term measures to cope.

This week on Zero, Akshat Rathi and Peter Guest explore the history of policy responses to energy shocks and what’s different in the 2020s.

Listen now, and subscribe on Apple, Spotify, or YouTube to get new episodes of Zero every Thursday.

Our transcripts are generated by a combination of software and human editors, and may contain slight differences between the text and audio. Please confirm in audio before quoting in print.

Akshat Rathi

Welcome to Zero, I am Akshat Rathi. This week: Asia's Ukraine moment.

It's been more than three months since the US attacked Iran, and Iran responded by closing the Strait of Hormuz. The world has moved beyond the idea that this is a short-term crisis, and many countries are now beginning to take longer-term measures to cope with the disruption to nearly 20% of the world's oil and gas supply that used to flow through the Strait.

I've been tracking what kind of measures countries are taking, and all that reporting, I'm pleased to say, is now a big feature for Bloomberg News, with great charts and photos that I hope you will read. We will link it in the show notes.

But as with any story, there's much more to talk about than what ends up on the page. This energy shock, after all, comes just four years after the other one that was precipitated by Russia's attack on Ukraine.

And dual shocks of this kind, like those in the 1970s, can have a deep impact on the energy system. So, to discuss the story, I have invited my editor, Peter Guest, to ask me the questions instead. Peter, welcome to the show.

Peter Guest

Thank you. So, Akshat, we're recording this May 27th, and things may change. But what's the state of the closure of the Strait of Hormuz?

Akshat Rathi

It is virtually closed. So, our colleagues here track the ships that go through the Strait of Hormuz. But the numbers are something like 90-95% closure. So, some ships still go through, but almost no oil and gas from the inside of the Strait is getting out these days. And it's been the case for the last three months.

Peter Guest

And even if the Strait was to open immediately, how serious would just that three months be?

Akshat Rathi

So, before the closure, there were about 100 ships going in and out on a daily basis. It's going to take months if the Strait was completely open for all the hundreds of ships that are trapped inside the Strait just to get out. And so the soonest you could see relief is months out. And if you look at the future price of oil, which is a good indicator of how the market thinks this disruption will play out, well, oil prices are not expected to fall below the $70 a barrel, which is what it was before the war until 2028.

Peter Guest

And it isn't just high prices, right? It's also volatile. These things have swung often just on the basis of a news report from the Iranian media or a Trump tweet.

Akshat Rathi

Yes. I mean, the last three months, you know, we could say the oil price has hovered around $100 a barrel. But really, a post from Trump on Truth Social will drop it 5-10% sometimes. And then a denial from the Iranian semi-official Fars News Agency will increase it again 2-4%. And it just happens far too often. But I think $100 is sort of a psychological number that plays on peoples’ minds that energy has become far too expensive to use.

Peter Guest

Given the scale of that disruption, though, why hasn't the price gone up even more?

Akshat Rathi

Yeah. So we had Jason Bordoff of Columbia University on the podcast one week after the war began, and he described the closure of the Strait as the ‘nightmare’ of all scenarios for the oil and gas markets. And he expected the price to go up. And since then, he's been giving many more interviews about this. And he's like, I am still surprised why the price has not gone up to $200 a barrel. Now, we know there are a few factors that have played a role to keep the price at only about $100 a barrel.

One is that before the war started, there was already more supply on the market than there was demand. So there was an expectation something like 3 million barrels a day higher supply this year relative to how much demand there was. There was a bunch of oil, sanctioned oil, that was floating on ships in the ocean, mostly Russian and Iranian oil. That oil was then unsanctioned by America during the weeks past. And much of that oil found buyers in Asia, including in India. And there are two pipelines, one from Saudi Arabia going to the Red Sea, which is not blocked, and one from the UAE going into the Arabian Sea, which has allowed for something like 5 to 7 million barrels of oil per day to get out.

Still, before the war, nearly 20 million barrels of oil per day was coming out. So it's still a pretty bad shock. But the price doesn't reflect it, and we keep getting told by analysts that at some point, this physical shortage will show up in price. Let's see.

Peter Guest

And as we say, the price isn't necessarily the deepest signal of what's going on. It contains a lot of other things. But how are we actually going to see supply shortages impact countries, economies, individuals, businesses around the world? Where are we seeing the greatest impact?

Akshat Rathi

As you know, the fossil fuel industry just has these boom and bust cycles. And they go through a high price and a low price environment pretty much cyclically. Almost every decade, there'll be a point where prices go up a lot. And countries, especially fuel importing countries, have developed a playbook on how to deal with high energy prices.

And so what we typically see is rich countries that have money to spend will try and help consumers not feel that shock by either cutting fuel taxes or directly subsidising fuels at some point. And poorer countries, which do not have that fiscal capacity, will typically put out notifications around being more frugal with your energy use. Try to work from home if you can. Make public transport cheaper for people to use. And we saw in the aftermath of the war, within the first few weeks, those types of immediate, short-term measures being put in place.

The International Energy Agency has put a policy tracker where it looks at all the countries that are doing it. And we put that in our story. And we see something like 80 countries have had some form of energy conservation measure or some form of pricing management measure. But these are short-term measures. And the longer this crisis lasts, the weaker their impact is on actually doing the job that they're supposed to do, which is to protect the consumer.

Peter Guest

And maybe talk me through some of those measures. What sort of things have we seen?

Akshat Rathi

So the Philippines is a good example, because it probably deployed some of the most number of these measures. They instituted a four-day work week for public officials. They encouraged that people limit the use of air conditioning to 24 degrees Celsius. They said all government officials should end any non-essential travel.

You know, in some cases, and this is not just the Philippines, but a few countries have declared a national energy emergency, allowing them to do things that in normal operation they won't be able to. The Philippines has also made bus rides free and has tried to encourage people to work from home if they can.

Peter Guest

I mean, these are quite short-term measures, right? As you say, there's a limited amount of fiscal space to keep on subsidising. So are we starting to see now three months in more comprehensive, longer-term shifts start to come into play?

Akshat Rathi

Yeah. So this is something that right on the weekend of the war is something that we in the Green team started discussing, because we experienced what happened when Russia attacked Ukraine and the policy response that came from European governments within weeks. And so this time we wanted to be prepared. Because if countries put policies in place, that has a much longer-term impact on how the energy system works.

So, you know, all of the team was looking for places where countries start to make these policy changes. And unlike in 2022, where there was a clear aggressor in Russia, and there was a clear dependence in Europe on Russia for all the fossil fuels they were importing, and there was some form of coordination within Europe, it took much longer for Asia to react in policy terms than it took Europe to react when the 2022 crisis played out. But we have seen policies come through.

So again, I'll bring up the example of the Philippines, because it's an example where it didn't just do the short-term things, it’s started thinking long-term. So it put out loan programmes for people to get rooftop solar and to get electric bikes. It had a bunch of renewable projects that it had approved, and some of them were even constructed. And it put out a diktat saying we will connect all these to the grid by the end of April 2026. Not all of them happened, but we know from on the ground reporting that many of them did get sped up as a result.

We also saw long-term measures in Europe, because despite the crisis hitting Asia the hardest this time around, European oil and gas prices have also gone up. So the EU came out with a large plan called AccelerateEU, which looks at trying to electrify the economy as fast as possible. But it's the European Union, they're putting out a statement, it's countries that have to put out the policies.

The first one to come out with a clear policy stance was France. And France has, for the past few years since the Ukraine crisis, actually has more electricity than it consumes. It's become one of the biggest exporters of electricity to the rest of Europe. And it would much prefer if that electricity was used domestically. And so it came out with a plan to try and increase the deployment of electricity consuming devices. So there are now subsidies that they already had on electric cars and on heat pumps, but they've been doubled in price.

They've also become more targeted. So new homes that are being built in France can no longer, as of the end of this year, have a gas boiler in them. They will have to come up with a heat pump. Similar policies were also brought in here in the UK, where we are, where new homes cannot have gas boilers now, they'll have to have heat pumps.

There's also been a push to try and increase the sale of electric cars. So we saw policy changes in Germany and Spain, where governments are speeding up the network for electric car charging. And that alongside the existing incentive that they have to buy electric cars, we've already seen electric car sales numbers skyrocket. So it is still early days, but we are starting to see policy change. And this policy change is what will really make a difference.

Peter Guest

So we'll come back to 2022, but in your piece you actually start further back and look at the energy shock that happened in the 1970s, and how structural changes happened then. Maybe come back to that comparison. First of all, what happened then? How did it reshape the energy mix?

Akshat Rathi

So the 1970s oil shock, two of them, one in 1973, because of the Yom Kippur War, and then the 1979 oil crisis was precipitated by the Iranian revolution. Both those shocks are talked about a lot because of the share of the global oil supply that was disrupted as a result.

So really until this energy crisis in 2026, no energy supply shock had been as large as the supply shocks in the 1970s. And I wanted to look at the 1970s because there is something powerful that happens when there are these deep shocks coming in quick succession. So ’73, ’79, six years apart, we have very oil dependent economies. And so what we saw after the 70s is a series of policy changes, both in America and Europe, that led to, for example, in the electricity mix, a rapid decline in oil consumption.

So countries like France and Japan put their government might behind building nuclear power plants. Nuclear had just started to become a real source of power for the world, Atoms for Peace programme that started after World War II. But really, France and Japan and the scale of their deployment, tens of reactors within a decade that were built, allowed nuclear to become cheap and more widely accessible. And oil disappeared from the European electricity mix within a decade.

In the US and in Canada, we saw some nuclear deployment, but also the return of coal. So oil had started to replace coal because oil was a better energy source and was cheaper. So they were both sort of positive things because you got a non-carbon source in nuclear, but a negative thing in that coal returned.

We also saw that countries used the moment to come up with fuel efficiency standards. So before the 70s, car makers could make whatever cars they wanted. But after the 70s, 1974 is when the US introduces the car fuel efficiency standards. Suddenly, within a decade, cars are essentially going twice the distance per gallon compared to the cars that you could buy in the 1970s. And the numbers are rather astonishing. US oil consumption actually declined in the 80s before it started to rise again because oil became cheap again in the 80s. But that policy had an impact not just in America, but in Europe too.

So Europe did not come up with fuel efficiency standards till the 90s. And yet, because of high fuel prices and the availability of more fuel efficient cars and smaller cars, even the mileage of European cars improved in that time. So to me, the 1970s were just an astonishing time to see how the energy mix can rapidly change when faced with a crisis. And I wanted to see if there were parallels from that time to now.

Peter Guest

And that change comes as a result of a combination of policy, consumer action, and technology, right? Fuel efficiency. This time around, it's going to be the same, right? But this time the technology is electric.

Akshat Rathi

Correct. This time, it is the same three steps, but more options to play with. So yes, you could, if you're a country so committed to nuclear, choose to move away from natural gas to nuclear. But really, right now, most countries are looking to an option that was not available in the 1970s, which is being able to build cheap solar and wind, and combine them with batteries. So on the electricity mix, there are more fuel switching choices available.

And then on efficiency side, it's not just trying to make your internal combustion engine, which is now 150 years old, that much more efficient. It is going electric. So now with an electric car, you can use the same unit of energy to go three times the distance compared to a fossil fuel car. And for the first time, these electric cars are affordable.

So even more than the 2022 shock, 2026 is a time when the number of car models that are available, the affordability of those car models, and the number of countries where we are seeing the sales is astonishingly large. So you get this difference in the 2020 shock, which allows you to have more options. And that is likely to reshape the energy system.

Peter Guest

And when we're talking about the economics of electrification and renewable energy, we're talking about China. China's changed the game. Maybe you can talk me through that a bit.

Akshat Rathi

So China's case, which we've talked about a lot on this podcast, is one that we typically say, oh, it's the world's largest deployer of these clean energy technologies, makes the most amount of solar and batteries and electric cars and makes it the cheapest. And does it not just for domestic consumption, which is huge, but really for exports and makes billions of dollars as a result of those exports. But those decisions took a long time to bear fruit.

And China was thinking about the closure of the Strait of Hormuz or other choke points, like the Strait of Malacca, which is close to Singapore, and what that might do to it. Because in a war, say with America, these choke points that used to be controlled by America could be shut. And suddenly this fossil fuel import dependent country that is China, which has coal, but really doesn't have much oil and gas, will have to find ways to get those fuels.

And so it started to do a bunch of things. One, it built a huge storage network for its oil. It also created huge numbers of plants that convert coal right now into chemicals, but can be really quickly switched to making liquid fuels or gaseous fuels, depending on whatever you want. But it also had these five-year plans to create new industries that would convert its usage of energy from fossil fuels to renewables. And that took a while, but it's paying off.

The energy prices in China compared to other economies haven't gone up that much. The government has been able to absorb a bunch of it. We had reporting in April, for example, that some of the Chinese oil companies were actually selling off their cargoes to European countries where they could make more money because they had plenty of oil for themselves at that time. It's a vindication of an energy security strategy, because that is how China has thought about this.

It's not really for climate goals. It's from an economic perspective and from a security perspective that it's worked out for them. And I think other countries may not be able to replicate what China has done, but can certainly take advantage and learn from what China has been able to do.

Akshat Rathi

After the break, Peter and I discuss whether this crisis is the kick up the backside the world needed to begin the transition away from fossil fuels. And hey, if you like what you're listening to and want to help support Zero, please take a couple of seconds, give us a rating, write us a review, and share this episode with someone else who you think might enjoy it. It helps new listeners find the show. Thank you.

Peter Guest

For so long, the conversation has been inflected very heavily by the idea of climate concerns. But what is really driving the shifts at a kind of macro scale is security. So let's look back at 2022, because we mentioned it earlier. Europe has been talking about shifting to renewable energy for a variety of reasons for some time. Then in February 2022, Russia launches a full scale invasion of Ukraine. Europe is very dependent on Russian gas and suddenly has a choice to make. So it's February, it's cold. What did they do?

Akshat Rathi

Well, we had within two weeks a plan from the International Energy Agency and then from the European Union itself, laying out the steps that they will take to wean the bloc from Russian fossil fuels. So about 40% of its natural gas came from piped natural gas from Russia, which is a huge amount. And so the first thing they did was try to find gas from other places.

There are some pipe gas networks in northern Africa, some in the Mediterranean, but that was only going to solve 5-10% of the problem. So they looked to the US, which had become the world's largest gas supplier, partly because of the 1970s, where one of the policies that the government invested in was to try and invest in the invention of fracking as we know it. So drilling for shale, and that has allowed America to become the world's largest producer of oil and gas. And so to go to America for some of its gas supplies through liquefied natural gas or to Qatar, which is the other large gas supplier.

Then it did the thing that we expect countries in the 2020s to do now, which is to look to other fuels like renewables and start to deploy them faster. So Europe already had one of the world's most ambitious climate goals and had targets to reach these renewables figures. But the thing that was holding it back was not money or target setting, it was bureaucracy. The EU, like most governments are bureaucratic, you have to go through a bunch of permitting processes, environmental assessments before you can deploy your renewables, solar or wind or even transmission network that is necessary.

And so we saw lots of countries really speed up the deployment. Germany is the example I reported on in 2023, which I think did the most. So we saw it pass laws that made the states in Germany dedicate 2% of their land to clean energy. They reduced the number of environmental assessments that need to be done for clean energy projects. They declared many clean energy projects as national security importance that allowed them to go through the bureaucracy faster. And suddenly, we could see in the numbers, there was a boom in solar in Europe in 2022 and 23. And then after that, there was in 23 and 24, a boom in battery deployment.

And within four years of these deployments, Europe is already taking advantage of this cheap power. So power prices in places like Spain and Portugal and Germany, and even in the UK, have not gone anywhere close to the levels that they had reached during the Ukraine crisis, in this Iran war crisis. So you know, we saw 300 and 400% increase in power prices in 2022. This time, they went up maybe 100% at one point, but for a very little period, and now they're about 50% higher for gas consumption today. So you can see that the playbook is changing. And countries are learning that the new technology set allows you to speed up the change in the energy system in ways that weren't possible in the past.

Peter Guest

And you say in the piece that this is Asia's Ukraine moment. So they're following the same pathway now.

Akshat Rathi

Yes, except that unlike Ukraine, Asia doesn't have one enemy. It's not like the Middle East is the enemy. And so there isn't as much coordination. And there isn't as much planning across governments as would happen in the EU. But we know there are blocks in Asia. So the ASEAN block is trying to build an interconnected grid that would allow countries to be able to trade a lot of their clean energy resources that they're starting to deploy.

We heard from world leaders, we heard from the South Korean president, from the Indonesian president, from the Vietnamese prime minister, all saying, we want more renewables, we want more electrification. We'll see if they're able to deploy it at the scale that Europe managed. These are not countries that have the same financial heft or planning ability that European countries have. But they do have something going for them.

So one of the analysts I spoke to at Ember, Dinita Setyawati, was talking about the fact that developing countries are building out their energy infrastructure. And it is easier to build new than it is to replace the old. So developed countries will struggle more, will have to pay more because they have to retire a bunch of things, whereas developing countries can choose to build clean and electric because they are growing their energy system. And with what China has been able to do, electrification now is also an economic opportunity for these countries. And that will drive that adoption way more than target setting or climate ambition.

Peter Guest

How do you think that argument will go down in Washington, D.C., where you've seen new hydrocarbons being made such a feature of the economic thinking and this almost quite aggressive rejection of the idea that renewable energy is incompatible with growth?

Akshat Rathi

We've seen, even before the Iran war, America not just domestically trying to scupper the deployment of clean energy under Donald Trump's second term, but also go out and try to fight on the international stage. So one of the things they did was fight this carbon tax that was going to be deployed on shipping, at the International Maritime Organisation. And it succeeded, that carbon tax is not coming as a result. But it's also turned these countries to think about energy in sovereign terms, something that America has been pushing forward, America first, right?

And for most of the fuel importing countries, when they put their interests first, they don't think, oh, yes, let's go to America to import more oil and gas. They think: well, we'll take America's oil and gas if we must, but we should try and deploy as much of domestic energy resources as we can, which tend to be renewables these days.

So we'll see how much America can continue to influence the energy system. You know, it's not like because of the state's closure, suddenly American oil companies are drilling, you know, 50 or 100 percent more to try and fill up the gap that's been created in the market. And so there is going to be a flux in the energy system in a way created by the shock that would be fun to report and observe in the years to come.

Peter Guest

We say flux, but there's something longer term here as well in the conduct of this war, in the way that it's proceeded, right? You have it in the piece that for a long time, America was the guarantor of energy security via the oil markets. And the state that the Strait of Hormuz is in, the state of the war, the way that it's played out and those wild swings we mentioned at the beginning suggest that maybe it no longer has that role. How profound is that for the energy system?

Akshat Rathi

Yeah, I spoke to Tim Sahay, who's a research scientist at Johns Hopkins University and focuses on climate and energy policies. And he said, hydrocarbons have now entered a different risk regime with the US unable to guarantee stable supplies from the Middle East. We thought of the US as the safety feature for shipping supplies. And if it is going to be the one that's blocking the Strait of Hormuz, as it has done now, how much security does fossil fuels have anymore?

These are fundamental questions that countries are going to have to ponder over and think about much more deeply than they've done before, because this energy crisis has just precipitated that decision moment for them sooner than they would have liked.

Peter Guest

But not just for countries, also for individuals. I think that's another feature of the story, is that as well as governments taking action, people are often moving ahead of their governments, motivated by very much the same things.

Akshat Rathi

Yeah. I mean, Tim also talks about, he calls it the hyperagency of the consumer. Unlike in the 1970s, where the energy system was this big system that governments and large corporations could change. Now, because a lot of the new energy, electrotech as it's called, is modular, and people can purchase it now at a supermarket. This changes the dynamic of how the energy system can switch over.

So some of the most fun reporting in the story was to actually talk to the people who did these switches for themselves. So one of them was a guy named Walter Serrano, who was a nurse in the Philippines. His annual salary is about $10,000. And he wanted to have his own energy, he said, but just couldn't afford it, because putting solar on the rooftop would have cost about $10,000 and that's his annual salary. And when the crisis happened, the Philippines came out with this programme for public workers, which he was, he worked at a government hospital, to get loans of up to $8,000. And he jumped at the opportunity and got it installed in the middle of April. And now he says his power bill and the loan repayment will be about half the price that he was paying in his power bills before.

So you're getting these individual cases that I think are fascinating to watch. I mean, some of them become real nerds in this. So another person I spoke to is Mark Purcell, who retired from the Australian Navy, and has now become a home electricity trader, because his utility named Amber allows him to be able to trade electricity at spot prices. Australia has some of the world's deepest penetration of rooftop solar. So about 40% of homes in Australia have rooftop solar.

And that causes power prices to swing quite wildly, especially when the sun goes down. And he's installed a bunch of batteries, and he decides when to store energy and when to put it back on the grid and say, sometimes I can make hundreds of dollars in the night. And he's become a community advocate converting his conservative, non-climate change believing Queenslanders to deploy solar and batteries in their homes. So I think that part of the energy transition that has come down to the individual level is fascinating.

Peter Guest

So if this moment is pushing retired Australian Navy veterans and Filipino nurses to take control of their own energy supplies and move to renewables, is this sort of finally the kick out the backside the world needed to actually make the transition away from fossil fuels happen?

Akshat Rathi

Well, oil companies were worried about this moment, because one of the things that they used to say about the energy transition is that they want an ‘orderly’ energy transition. That governments decide what the energy mix is and how it changes and do so slowly.

But as these crises show, the energy transition is not going to be a smooth, simple curve, and it's not going to be orderly. Do we need a crisis for the transition to move forward? I hope not. But now that it's here, it's interesting to watch what happens.

Peter Guest

I don't think we're going to be short of crises.

Akshat Rathi

Well, that will keep us busy as journalists. Thank you, Peter.

Peter Guest

Thanks alot, Akshat.

Akshat Rathi

And thank you for listening to Zero. Now for the sound of the week

President Dwight Eisenhower

The atomic age has moved forward at such a pace that every citizen of the world should have some comprehension, at least in comparative terms, of the extent of this development, of the utmost significance to every one of us.

Akshat Rathi

That is US President Eisenhower giving his Atoms for Peace speech in 1953, which kick started the development of nuclear power for civilian use.

If you liked this episode, please take a moment to rate and review the show on Apple Podcasts, YouTube and Spotify. This episode was produced by Oscar Boyd. Our theme music is composed by Wonderly. Special thanks to all the people who worked on the story with me: Neil Jerome Morales, Hayley Warren, Ewa Krukowska, Stephen Stapczynski, Francois de Beaupuy, Petra Sorge, Heesu Lee, Sam Dodge, Sommer Saadi, Laura Millan and Sharon Chen. I'm Akshat Rathi, back soon.
item.dc:creator: Akshat Rathi, Peter Guest, Oscar Boyd

item.title: Trump’s Arctic Refuge Auction to Test Oil Industry’s Appetite
item.description:
President Donald Trump vowed to reopen Alaska’s Arctic National Wildlife Refuge to oil drilling as he seeks to make tapping the state’s vast natural resources a key part of his energy agenda.

Now comes the test of the oil industry’s appetite to drill in the rugged frozen tundra, long prized for its oil and gas potential but burdened by political uncertainty, environmental opposition and logistical challenges. The Trump administration is opening sealed bids for nearly 690,000 acres of oil and gas drilling rights in the refuge’s coastal plain starting at 10 a.m. Alaska time on Friday.

The sale is the Interior Department’s first in the refuge since lifting drilling restrictions imposed by former President Joe Biden. It’s also the first of four lease sales required under Trump’s tax-and-spending law, the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, which mandates at least four auctions in the area by 2035.

Trump signed legislation during his first term ending a four-decade ban on energy development in the refuge, which is estimated to contain as much as 11.8 billion barrels of recoverable oil. The protected area occupies a section of northeastern Alaska roughly the size of South Carolina.

Major oil companies largely steered clear of the two lease sales that followed. An auction held weeks before Trump left office in January 2021 drew bids from two oil developers and Alaska’s state-owned economic development company. A second sale in January 2025 under the Biden administration resulted in zero bids, though oil industry representatives and Alaska officials argued restrictive lease terms artificially discouraged interest.

Some analysts are skeptical.

“Production will be challenging and the prospect that permits will be canceled as soon as a new administration takes over is likely given that this is an easy way for a new administration to make a show of its environmental commitment,” said Ellen Wald, a senior fellow with the Atlantic Council Global Energy Center and the president of Transversal Consulting. “I wouldn’t be surprised if we see greater interest now than we would have had this lease sale taken place in January.”

Still, a March lease sale in the National Petroleum Reserve in Alaska drew a record $163 million in bids from established operators like ConocoPhillips, as well as companies such as Exxon Mobil Corp., which last drilled an exploratory well in the state in the early 1990s. Shell Plc, which abandoned Arctic exploration after a costly unsuccessful search for crude waters north of Alaska, partnered with Repsol SA to secure over 40 leases.

Read more: Alaska’s Oil Revival Draws Energy Industry Back to the Arctic

Among those that have indicated interest in Friday’s sale is the Alaska Industrial Development and Export Authority, the state’s economic development agency. The authority was the top bidder in the refuge’s 2021 lease sale and recently authorized millions of dollars to participate again. The other participants in the 2021 auction, Knik Arm Services LLC and Regenerate Alaska Inc., each secured one lease.

Environmental groups and some indigenous communities, including Gwich’in, which consider the coastal plain sacred, argue drilling threatens Arctic foxes, polar bears, caribou, musk oxen and migratory birds.

“The diverse and sacred landscape of the Arctic Refuge is unlike any other and should never be sacrificed for oil and gas drilling,” said America Fitzpatrick, a program director with the League of Conservation Voters. “Any companies considering drilling in the Arctic Refuge would be doing so against the majority of people who support protecting this critical landscape.”

Meanwhile, local leaders, including those in Kaktovik, the only village within the refuge, support development, arguing it’s necessary for the region’s economic well-being.

Read more: Trump Reopens Oil Drilling Alaska Arctic Wildlife Refuge

Alaska crude production has steadily declined from a peak of 2 million barrels a day in 1988 to roughly 417,000 barrels a day in March, according to the Energy Information Administration. The agency projects output will rise to 450,000 barrels a day this year and 500,000 barrels a day in 2027 as new developments begin producing.
item.dc:creator: Ari Natter
